author: @TimeSurfer206
text:
"The best" is the one that gives me an acceptable efficiency, with an acceptable dependability, for an acceptable price.
The greater the first two are, the more I might be inclined to accept a greater expense.
_PROVIDED:_ That added expense is justified by an increased return.

author: @timeorspace
text: Robert….Thank You!  I disappointed a friend yesterday, who asked if I was doing engineering work.  My friend was saddened to find out I am not pursuing engineering or tech jobs, but instead entering the service industry.  I’ve been a part of some amazing scientific projects, but it has not been easy, lucrative, or family friendly.  Even though I’ve personally invested in & sacrificed for my technical education, no professor taught me I would be asked to volunteer my mind to fight my belief-essentially ripping my soul into pieces.  After working in various industries ranging sewer utilities to electro-chemical experiments on Mars, my sense tells me that technology has out-paced philosophy.  Without wise leadership, more ‘efficiency or profit’ can be a greater force for harm than for good.  “Beauty walks a razor’s edge, someday I’ll make it mine”. - Bob Dylan

author: @Marian_Martin_Morgenstern
text:
The "Best One" also shows the single-mindedness about that there needs to be a "one-and-only" solution to everything. A Panacea that will cure every ailment.
The world doesn't work that way, every niche forms ist own set of problems, which in turn needs its own set of solutions. And some of them will conflict with each other, so you have to make a choice which one you want to apply... even IF its not the most efficient one per se, but the one most fitting for your problem

author: @davidblyth5495
text:
Your statement "According to my view!" is valid! Each application has its own requirements.

In my work I found that the ISO/IEC 15288 Systems and software engineering — System life cycle processes was a useful yardstick that avoided "efficiency" or other localised yardsticks.
In other words how well does it satisfy the requirement over its total life-cycle.
That takes into consideration efficiency, operating costs, environmental effects, logistics (cost, producibility, maintainability, upgradability, etc), procurement costs, etc

author: @lajosruhzek2459
text: Efficiency is important, but I think the environment that it is being built for is the most important. In low wind speeds, something like the Darwin Wind Tower that you've been working on is probably better than any traditional HAWT. In terms of swept area the Darwin Wind Tower will almost certainly have a far worse efficiency since each level of the tower is harvesting wind making it effectively part of the swept area of the blade, but it will have an ultimately smaller turbine for it. But because of the collected wind, it'll be much easier to turn than a traditional HAWT, and thus operate better under lower wind speeds. So something like the Darwin Wind Tower might be more appropriate for the city, while a big HAWT might be what is appropriate for an industrial scale wind farm.

author: @anotherdave5107
text: Best! Best! You're giving me deja vu nightmares! I interviewed for an electrical  engineering position at a large corporation decades ago. I talked to each engineer in the department in turn until lastly introduced to an elderly engineer with a thick slavic accent. He wanted to talk about inductors. He asked me what was the BEST inductor. I said it depends on how best was defines due to the situation. If cost is the driver then a simple copper coil if it can meet the specs may ne the answer. If size was the primary goal then perhaps a winding on a ferrite core. As I continued he threw up his hands and and began to scream best! best!. I excused myself and went to dept manager and declined to persue the position.

author: @paulmurgatroyd6372
text: It's interesting that people have started doing trials with the beehive style cone deflection turbines, it's likely these are simpler and easier to maintain than a similar sized HAWT.  It's a good bet that these qualities are more important in the long run than simple generator efficiency, especially for non-critical applications.

author: @brianknowles7130
text:
I use to inspect the HV side of about 200 - 300 vertical wind turbines in England, Wales, Scotland each month. All of the sites have a ' Met mast' [ meteorological mast ] with a anemometer fitted on the top of it always searching for the best wind direction for the turbines to face. Obviously computer controlled. Blades and generator at the top and HV transformer at the bottom connected via HV cables. Should the turbine/s continue to turn in the same direction for more than 2.5 turns, a shear pin would be ' pulled' automatically and stop the cables becoming ' cork screwed'. Won't bore you with more details
other than to say 'the grid ' can't take what they can sometimes supply so are tripped. Such a waste of energy me thinks !

replies.author: @kadmow
replies.text: - not boring at all... But if you only build nameplate capacity there will be many times when the grid is lacking supply, that is the rub with intermittency - surpkus is required some of the time.  The cost isn't for the "capacity" it is for the need to overbuilt in order to ensure supply, that factor is still up for debate. (The costs are rarely discussed for a firm grid, today.)

replies.author: @brianknowles7130
replies.text: @kadmow As Rob has aptly shown, it's easy to make electricity, the problem is storing it any any great quantity for use when needed. What to do with surplus electricity ..? Just another ' throw away Q' .. do the grid/s want electricity from sources they don't own ..? Hhmm . you and I do, but ...maybe getting a bit off topic here eh ..lol ..

replies.author: @kadmow
replies.text:
@brianknowles7130  - Where I live the Grid isn't owned by the retailers. Not to say the market system in place is perfect, while retailers may have generation "branches" the transmission system is owned by an independent authority - so the power on the grid isn't influenced by any entity - but market forces..

I am geared to mostly use my own generation and storage at home - except for transport, EVs aren't in my allowable budget, and I prefer not to pander to my own vanity (not economical)..  lol_

So we have a 2-fold problem, intermittency and inability to store surplus - these are unlikely to be solves in a simple way, so we will continue to pay for surplus generation (presently cheaper than firming the grid with storage - outside of the perfect world.)

Have a great day.

replies.author: @K10wNs-Shed
replies.text:
Or more so, the one that keeps working.

I've played around with both vertical axis and horizontal axis and the bearings taking so much load on the vertical ones meant they would stop functioning (seize the bearing) in a shorter time frame.  The horizontal axis one has outlived the other by 3 years and still going strong.  (I'm off-grid on solar and wind).

replies.author: @bobroberts8500
replies.text: @K10wNs-Shed did your vertical windmills only connect at the bottom?

replies.author: @K10wNs-Shed
replies.text:
@bobroberts8500 Mounted from both the top and bottom on tapered bearings.  I guess that keeping the weather out of the bearings was also an issue and contributed to the failure in less time than expected.

Using a magnetic bearing to hold the weight might be a solution.  Not sure it would be able to hold the weight of such a large structure though.
